title: Geometry selective colossal negative dielectric permittivity in CaFe2O4 nanostructures
abstract: Negative permittivity metamaterial is a scientifically rich avenue due to its tremendous application in several arena of materials research including novel superlens, band-gap materials, invisibility cloaks, antenna and filter design. Traditionally, epsilon negative (ENG) behaviour is achieved in multi-phase composites with the addition of conducting metal fillers. However, this study reports colossal ENG feature in a single phase Calcium Ferrite for a particular nano hollow spherical (NHS) morphology, without the use of any filler. On the contrary, the same material synthesized in a different morphology, namely, nano solid sphere (NSS) shows conventional dielectric behaviour. Occurrence of ENG is successfully interpreted with the phase inversion of dominant polarization within the hollow cavity of NHS. This report marks a significant step in realizing colossal ENG in a single phase material just by restructuring the nanoscale morphology.
summary: This paper reports a method to achieve colossal negative permittivity in a single-phase material, CaFe2O4, by simply changing its nanostructure from solid to hollow spheres. Unlike traditional metamaterials that require adding metal fillers, the hollow geometry itself induces a phase inversion of polarization. This effect is driven by charge accumulation at the inner cavity boundary, creating a net polarization opposite to the applied electric field. This discovery suggests that morphology engineering can be a powerful tool for designing novel dielectric materials for applications like superlenses and antennas.
main_problem: Achieving epsilon-negative (ENG) behavior in single-phase materials without the use of conductive metallic fillers, which are traditionally required in multi-phase composites.
main_result: The study demonstrates that nano hollow spherical (NHS) morphology of Calcium Ferrite (CaFe2O4) induces colossal negative permittivity due to a phase inversion of polarization within the hollow cavity, a feature absent in solid nano spheres.
method: The researchers used a template-free solvothermal method for synthesis and characterized the morphology using XRD, TEM, and SAED, while analyzing dielectric properties via frequency and temperature-dependent measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Calcium Ferrite (CaFe2O4) nanostructures in two morphologies: Nano Solid Spheres (NSS) and Nano Hollow Spheres (NHS), studied in the temperature range of 298 K to 573 K and frequency range up to several MHz.
key_observables: Relative permittivity (epsilon'), AC conductivity, phase shift angle (Phi), and activation energy (Ea).
important_parameters: Temperature (298 K–570 K), frequency (kHz–MHz range), and morphology (hollow vs. solid).
main_assumptions: The mechanism assumes that the polarization within the air-filled cavity partially offsets the polarization in the shell, leading to a net polarization opposite to the applied electric field.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows TEM and SAED for morphology and crystallinity; Fig 2 shows frequency dependence of AC conductivity and Arrhenius plots for activation energy; Fig 3 shows frequency dependence of permittivity for NSS and NHS; Fig 4 provides a schematic of the polarization inversion mechanism; Fig 5 shows the frequency variation of the phase shift angle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ENG materials, describes the synthesis and characterization of the two morphologies, presents the comparative dielectric and conductivity measurements, proposes a physical mechanism based on polarization inversion in the hollow cavity, and concludes with the significance of morphology engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: TEM micrographs of CaFO (a) NSS, and (d) NHS. The thick shell and hollow cavity are visible for NHS. (220) and (311) crystallographic planes with the corresponding interplanar distances are shown for (b) NSS and (e) NHS, respectively. The SAED patterns identified with different crystallographic planes are shown for (c) NSS and (f) NHS.

Fig. 2: Frequency (f) variation of ac conductivity of CaFO (a) NSS, and (b) NHS at different temperatures, T. The adjacent colour bar indicates the colour code for different T. The corresponding fits according to Jonscher’s law (equation (1)) are shown in solid black or grey line for NSS and cyan line for NHS. The law is valid only up to certain f for all T for NSS and T up to 373 K for NHS. Corresponding insets show Arrhenius plot (natural logarithm of dc conductivity vs. inverse of temperature). The solid red line indicates linear fitting.

Fig. 3: Frequency variation of real part of relative permittivity, ′, of CaFO (a) NSS, and (b) NHS at different temperatures, T. The adjacent colour bar indicates the colour code for different T. The inset of (a), and (b) show the enlarged portions of the corresponding curves for NSS and NHS, respectively, near ′ = 0. A solid black horizontal line at ′ = 0 is drawn for a guide to the eye.

Fig. 4: Schematic interpretation of the corresponding polarization mechanism for (a) NSS and (b) NHS. The black and red arrows indicate the direction of the applied electric field and the polarization vector developed in the material. The larger arrow for polarization in central cavity (𝑃𝑐⃗⃗⃗ ) than that of the shell region (𝑃𝑠⃗⃗⃗ ) indicates stronger polarization in the cavity than the shell area for NHS.

Fig. 5: Frequency variation of phase shift angle, , of CaFO (a) NSS, and (b) NHS at different temperatures, T. For clarity,  corresponding to some select T only are shown, as indicated by the colour code. A solid black horizontal line at  = 0 is drawn for a guide to the eye.
